sedentary.label: Sedentary
sedentary.description: Inactive adults (≤2 sessions/week unstructured activity). Focus: functional capacity, fall-risk screening, ADL performance. ACSM health-related fitness norms.
sedentary.scoring_anchor: 5/10 = minimum functional standard. <5 = screening flag; clinician review recommended.
references: ACSM Guidelines for Exercise Testing and Prescription, 12th Ed.
Rikli & Jones (2013) Senior Fitness Test Manual, 2nd Ed.
Blackwell JR et al. (2020) AJOT — DYNAVISION D2 norms, adults 18–80 yr
NIH Toolbox Cognition Battery V3 Norming (PMC 2025)

rows.protocol: 3 CMJ trials. Hands on hips. 60 s rest. Impulse-momentum method preferred over flight time. Best trial recorded.
rows.notes: ACSM/NSCA norms sedentary adults. M ~22 cm; F ~14 cm. Sayers et al. (1999); NSCA 2024.

rows.metric: CMJ Peak Force (relative to body weight)
rows.protocol: Peak GRF (N) ÷ body weight (N = mass × 9.81). Best of 3 CMJ trials. Hands on hips.
rows.notes: Typical peak GRF ~1.5–1.9× BW in sedentary adults. <12 N/kg = functional weakness flag. Linthorne (2001); McMahon et al. (2018).

rows.protocol: Eccentric RFD extracted from force-time curve braking phase. Mean of 2 best CMJ trials.
rows.notes: Reflects braking strength and SSC loading quality. McMahon et al. (2018).

rows.metric: IMTP Peak Force (relative to body mass)
rows.equipment: STRENGTHSenses GRFP Force Plates + Isometric Rig
rows.protocol: 2 familiarisation trials @ 50–75% effort. 2–3 max trials × 3–5 s. Hip 145°, knee ~120°. Net peak force ÷ body mass. Best trial.
rows.notes: Dos'Santos et al. (2017); Haff et al. (2015). <10 N/kg = functional weakness flag. Net force = peak − BW.

rows.metric: IMTP Rate of Force Development (0–100 ms)
rows.equipment: STRENGTHSenses GRFP Force Plates + Isometric Rig
rows.protocol: Force-time curve. 100 ms window from onset. Onset threshold = 75 N above body weight. Mean of 2 best trials.
rows.notes: Early RFD window. Khamoui et al. (2011); Haff et al. (2015); NSCA 2024.

rows.protocol: Dual force plates. Symmetry% = 100 − (|L−R| / max(L,R)) × 100. Measured during CMJ. Mean of 3 trials.
rows.notes: <85% symmetry = clinical concern (i.e. >15% asymmetry). Impellizzeri et al. (2007). CMJ preferred over IMTP for functional assessment.

rows.protocol: Seated, elbow 90°, shoulder neutral. 3 trials × 3 s maximal squeeze. Best trial recorded.
rows.notes: ACSM; Norman et al. (2011). Dynapenia threshold: <20 kg (F), <26 kg (M) — Malmstrom et al. (2016).

rows.protocol: Grip absolute (kg) ÷ body mass (kg).
rows.notes: ACSM HGS normalisation (2024). More meaningful for inter-individual comparison than absolute.

rows.protocol: 10 m walk test. Comfortable self-selected pace. 2 trials. Mean recorded.
rows.notes: PubMed spatiotemporal norms (2024). <0.8 m/s = fall-risk threshold (ACSM). Strong predictor of adverse health outcomes.

rows.protocol: Extracted from 10 m walk at self-selected speed. Mean of ≥6 consecutive strides.
rows.notes: PubMed spatiotemporal norms (2024). Shorter strides = mobility deficit or fear of falling.

rows.protocol: Steps per minute during 10 m walk. Mean of 2 trials.
rows.notes: Spatiotemporal norms (2024). Low cadence combined with low gait speed amplifies fall risk.

rows.protocol: T-Mode. All 5 ring zones. 60 s. Seated if limited standing capacity, otherwise standing. 1 practice + 1 scored trial.
rows.notes: Blackwell et al. (2020) AJOT. Adults 18–40 yr seated median ~460 ms; standing ~430 ms. Expected >550 ms for deconditioned populations.

rows.protocol: Timed Up and Go (TUG) or standardised reach task. Captured at ≥100 Hz. SPARC extracted from wrist/hand segment velocity profile.
rows.notes: Morin & Samozino (2016). Less negative = smoother movement. Sedentary populations show high jerk due to poor motor control.

rows.protocol: Submaximal ramp (15–20 W/min) or YMCA step protocol. Extrapolate to age-predicted HRmax. Direct maximal test at clinician discretion if medically cleared.
rows.notes: ACSM 12th Ed. Sedentary adult norms (M/F mixed). Poor <24; Fair 24–30; Good 30–37. <18 = referral consideration.

rows.protocol: Identified from V-slope method or ventilatory equivalents (VE/VO2, VE/VCO2) during ramp CPET. First systematic rise in VE/VO2 without corresponding rise in VE/VCO2.
rows.notes: Zone 2 training anchor (~55–60% VO2max). Buchheit & Laursen (2013).

rows.protocol: Record peak exercise HR and HR at exactly 60 s post cessation. Passive recovery (seated). HRR1 = peak HR − HR at 60 s.
rows.notes: Cole et al. (1999) NEJM. ACSM. <12 bpm = autonomic dysfunction; referral consideration.

rows.protocol: 5 min supine rest. Record HR over final 2 min. Mean of final 30 readings.
rows.notes: ACSM. >90 bpm resting = referral consideration. Sedentary adults typically 65–80 bpm.

rows.protocol: Tablet-based Pattern Comparison Processing Speed Test. Standard administration procedure. Age- and education-corrected T-score (mean=50, SD=10) then converted to standard score (mean=100).
rows.notes: NIH Toolbox CB V3 Norming (2025). Sedentary adults trend 5–10 pts below population mean. Sensitive to physical activity levels.

rows.protocol: Flanker Inhibitory Control & Attention Test. Congruent and incongruent arrow conditions. Age-corrected composite standard score.
rows.notes: NIH Toolbox CB V3 (2025). Inhibitory control declines with inactivity; sensitive to aerobic fitness level.

rows.protocol: D2 T-Mode (all rings, 60 s) while simultaneously performing serial subtraction (−3 from 100). Mean hit time recorded.
rows.notes: Blackwell et al. (2020). Expected dual-task cost ~20–40% worse than simple RT in sedentary population.

rows.protocol: DTC% = ((Dual RT − Single RT) / Single RT) × 100. Derived from same-session simple and dual-task RT measures.
rows.notes: High DTC% = executive control impairment. Strobach et al. (2012). Sedentary adults show elevated DTC vs trained.

trained.label: Trained
trained.description: Recreationally active adults, 3–5 sessions/week structured training. Performance-health continuum norms.
trained.scoring_anchor: 7/10 = recreational athlete standard. <6 = training gap detected.
references: Kenney, Wilmore & Costill (2020) Physiology of Sport and Exercise, 7th Ed.
McMahon JJ et al. (2018) Strength Cond J — CMJ force-time phases
Dos'Santos T et al. (2017) JSCR — IMTP onset-threshold methods
Blackwell JR et al. (2020) DYNAVISION D2 norms

rows.protocol: 3 CMJ trials. Hands on hips. 60 s rest. Impulse-momentum method. Best trial.
rows.notes: Trained adults. M ~33–40 cm; F ~24–32 cm. Sayers et al. (1999); Gathercole et al. (2015).

rows.metric: CMJ Peak Force (relative to body weight)
rows.protocol: Peak GRF (N) ÷ body weight (N). Best of 3 CMJ trials. Hands on hips.
rows.notes: Typical peak GRF ~1.8–2.2× BW in trained recreational adults. Linthorne (2001); McMahon et al. (2018).

rows.protocol: Eccentric RFD from braking phase of force-time curve. Mean of 2 best CMJ trials.
rows.notes: Braking RFD reflects SSC efficiency and stretch-shortening loading capacity. McMahon et al. (2018).

rows.protocol: Net concentric impulse (N·s) from force-time curve. Interval: zero velocity to takeoff (net GRF > 10 N).
rows.notes: Impulse-momentum theorem: jump height ∝ net impulse. McMahon et al. (2018).

rows.metric: IMTP Peak Force (relative to body mass)
rows.equipment: STRENGTHSenses GRFP Force Plates + Isometric Rig
rows.protocol: 2 familiarisation @ 50–75%. 3 max trials × 3–5 s. Hip 145°, knee ~120°. Net peak force ÷ body mass. Best trial.
rows.notes: Trained recreational. Dos'Santos et al. (2017); Comfort et al. (2019). Relative preferred over absolute.

rows.equipment: STRENGTHSenses GRFP Force Plates + Isometric Rig
rows.protocol: Force-time curve. 100 ms window from onset (threshold 75 N above BW). Mean of 2 best trials.
rows.notes: Khamoui et al. (2011); NSCA 2024 — trained recreational athletes.

rows.protocol: SI% = (|L−R| / max(L,R)) × 100. Dual plates during CMJ. Mean of 3 trials.
rows.notes: >12% = injury risk flag. Impellizzeri et al. (2007). CMJ preferred for functional asymmetry.

rows.protocol: Seated, elbow 90°. 3 trials × 3 s. Best trial. Compare dominant to non-dominant.
rows.notes: ACSM. Trained adults ~15–20% above sedentary norms. Dynapenia: <26 kg (M), <20 kg (F).

rows.protocol: Grip absolute (kg) ÷ body mass (kg).
rows.notes: ACSM HGS normalisation (2024).

rows.protocol: Lateral pinch between thumb and index. Elbow 90°. 3 trials. Best trial.
rows.notes: Sport-specific relevance for racket, climbing, overhead throwing sports.

rows.protocol: 10 m walk test, comfortable pace. 2 trials averaged.
rows.notes: Spatiotemporal gait norms (PubMed 2024). Trained adults 10–20% faster than sedentary.

rows.protocol: Mean of ≥6 strides from 10 m walk.
rows.notes: Spatiotemporal norms (2024). Reflects limb mobility and coordination.

rows.protocol: Treadmill run at comfortable jogging speed (~9–12 km/h). Mean of ≥10 steps after 2 min familiarisation.
rows.notes: Lower GCT = better elastic energy utilisation and leg stiffness. Applies to running, not walking.

rows.protocol: T-Mode, 60 s, all 5 rings. Standing. 1 practice + 2 scored trials. Best result recorded.
rows.notes: Blackwell et al. (2020). Trained adults 18–40 yr: median ~300–330 ms standing.

rows.protocol: Overhead reach or sport-specific movement task at self-selected speed. SPARC extracted from wrist segment velocity.
rows.notes: Morin & Samozino (2016). Trained adults show less jerk than sedentary due to improved motor patterning.

rows.protocol: Ramp protocol 20–25 W/min. Direct VO2 measurement preferred. Plateau criteria: ≤2.1 mL/kg/min increase with ↑load, RER ≥1.10.
rows.notes: ACSM 12th Ed. Trained adults (M/F mixed). Good: 41–52; Excellent: >52.

rows.protocol: V-slope method. First systematic rise in VE/VO2 without concurrent rise in VE/VCO2.
rows.notes: Typically 55–65% VO2max in trained adults. Zone 2 upper boundary. Buchheit & Laursen (2013).

rows.protocol: Second inflection: rise in both VE/VO2 and VE/VCO2. Approximates maximal lactate steady state.
rows.notes: Typically 75–85% VO2max. Key performance intensity marker. Buchheit & Laursen (2013).

rows.protocol: Peak HR − HR at 60 s post cessation. Passive seated recovery.
rows.notes: Marker of autonomic (vagal) recovery. Cole et al. (1999). Trained adults recover faster than sedentary.

rows.protocol: Standard age- and education-corrected administration.
rows.notes: Exercise-cognition literature shows +5–10 SS vs sedentary. NIH Toolbox CB V3 (2025).

rows.protocol: T-Mode, 60 s. Standing. 2 scored trials. Best result.
rows.notes: Blackwell et al. (2020). Shared norm with movement domain — same physical assessment.

rows.protocol: DTC% = ((Dual RT − Single RT) / Single RT) × 100.
rows.notes: Trained individuals show significantly lower DTC than sedentary. Strobach et al. (2012).

competitive.label: Competitive
competitive.description: Competitive athletes with structured sport-specific programming, competing at regional–national level. Performance database norms.
references: NSCA Essentials of Strength Training & Conditioning, 4th Ed.
Dos'Santos T et al. (2017) JSCR — IMTP onset-threshold methods
McMahon JJ et al. (2018) Strength Cond J — CMJ phase analysis
Flanagan EP & Comyns TM (2008) Strength Cond J — RSI
Laursen PB & Buchheit M (2019) Science & Application of HIIT

rows.protocol: 3 CMJ trials. Hands on hips. 90 s rest. Impulse-momentum method. Best trial.
rows.notes: Competitive athletes (M/F mixed). M: 44–54 cm; F: 34–44 cm. Gathercole et al. (2015); NSCA 2024.

rows.metric: CMJ Peak Force (relative to body weight)
rows.protocol: Peak GRF (N) ÷ body weight (N). Best of 3 CMJ trials.
rows.notes: Typical ~2.0–2.5× BW in competitive athletes. Linthorne (2001); Gathercole et al. (2015).

rows.protocol: Eccentric RFD from braking phase. Mean of 2 best trials.
rows.notes: Key SSC loading marker. McMahon et al. (2018). Sensitive to fatigue and training status.

rows.protocol: Net concentric impulse from CMJ force-time curve. Takeoff threshold: 10 N.
rows.notes: Impulse-momentum theorem. r=0.97 correlation with jump height. JSCR.

rows.protocol: Drop jump from 30 cm box. Minimal knee flexion. RSI = jump height (m) ÷ GCT (s). Best of 3 trials. Rest 90 s between.
rows.notes: Reactive SSC capacity. Flanagan & Comyns (2008); Beattie et al. (2017). >2.0 m/s = high-performance standard.

rows.metric: IMTP Peak Force (relative to body mass)
rows.equipment: STRENGTHSenses GRFP Force Plates + Isometric Rig
rows.protocol: 2 familiarisation + 3 max trials × 3–5 s. Hip 145°, knee ~120°. Net peak force ÷ body mass. Best trial.
rows.notes: Dos'Santos et al. (2017); Comfort et al. (2019). >30 N/kg = high-performance threshold.

rows.equipment: STRENGTHSenses GRFP Force Plates + Isometric Rig
rows.protocol: 100 ms window from onset (75 N above BW). Mean of 2 best trials.
rows.notes: Correlated with sprint speed and CMJ height. NSCA 2024.

rows.equipment: STRENGTHSenses GRFP Force Plates + Isometric Rig
rows.protocol: 200 ms window from force-time curve. Complementary to 0–100 ms window.
rows.notes: Captures late explosive capacity. Haff et al. (2015).

rows.protocol: SI% = (|L−R| / max(L,R)) × 100. Dual plates during CMJ. Mean of 3 trials.
rows.notes: >10% = injury risk investigation. Impellizzeri et al. (2007). Monitor longitudinally for change >5%.

rows.protocol: Seated. 3 trials each hand. Best trial.
rows.notes: Competitive athletes. Overhead/racket sport specific relevance. ACSM norms.

rows.protocol: Multi-directional push/pull movements. 3 planes × 3 trials. Mean power recorded.
rows.notes: 3D resistive force — sport-specific power profiling. Benchmark by sport.

rows.metric: Ground Contact Time (running at competition pace)
rows.protocol: Treadmill at race/competition pace. Mean of ≥10 steps after 2 min familiarisation.
rows.notes: Lower GCT = greater leg stiffness and elastic energy return. Haugen et al. (2020).

rows.protocol: At competition pace on treadmill. Mean of ≥10 strides.
rows.notes: Sport- and speed-dependent. Sprinters >2.0 m; endurance ~1.50–1.70 m.

rows.protocol: Single-leg squat to 60° knee flexion. Bilateral. Knee valgus angle and hip drop compared L vs R. Mean of 3 trials each side.
rows.notes: >8° asymmetry = injury risk flag. 3D joint angles via Noraxon Ultium.

rows.protocol: T-Mode, 60 s, all 5 rings. Standing. 2 scored trials. Best.
rows.notes: Competitive athletes show faster RT than trained. Blackwell et al. (2020).

rows.protocol: Sport-specific movement sequence at competition speed. SPARC extracted from wrist segment.
rows.notes: Significantly smoother than sedentary. Morin & Samozino (2016).

rows.protocol: Surface EMG on quadriceps, hamstrings, glutes during reactive perturbation or drop-landing task. Onset latency from perturbation to muscle activation. Mean of 5 trials.
rows.notes: Earlier onset = better neuromuscular pre-activation. Correlated with injury prevention.

rows.protocol: Individualised ramp or Bruce protocol. RER ≥1.10 + HR within 10 bpm of age-predicted max as confirmation criteria. Plateau verification.
rows.notes: ACSM excellent range + team sport databases. Team sport athletes typically 52–65. Squeo et al. (2025).

rows.protocol: VT2 = second deflection in ventilatory equivalents. Approximates critical speed / race pace.
rows.notes: Typically 80–88% VO2max in competitive athletes. Buchheit & Laursen (2013).

rows.protocol: Peak HR − HR at 60 s post (passive seated recovery).
rows.notes: Indicator of cardiac vagal reactivation. Cole et al. (1999). Athletes recover significantly faster.

rows.metric: Submaximal HR at Fixed Work Rate
rows.protocol: HR at 150 W (M) or 100 W (F). 5 min steady-state. Track longitudinally within training blocks.
rows.notes: Lower HR at fixed load = greater cardiac efficiency. Sensitive fatigue monitoring metric. Buchheit (2014).

rows.protocol: Pattern Comparison Processing Speed. Age/education corrected.
rows.notes: Competitive athletes show superior processing speed vs sedentary. NIH Toolbox CB V3 (2025).

rows.protocol: Random mode + peripheral lights. 60 s. Mean hit time.
rows.notes: Competitive athletes: trained cohort norms. Blackwell et al. (2020).

rows.protocol: D2 + sport-relevant decision task (e.g. colour identification). DTC% derived.
rows.notes: Competitive athletes show superior executive control under dual-task loading.

rows.protocol: Seated. n-back (2-back) or Stroop task. ΔHbO measured over prefrontal cortex channels. 3 min task block.
rows.notes: Positive ΔHbO = prefrontal cortex engagement. Scholkmann et al. (2014); Artinis Brite MKIV has device-validation literature, but threshold applicability remains under audit.

elite.label: Elite
elite.description: Elite/professional athletes at national–international level. High-volume structured programming. Marginal gains logic — small deltas are clinically meaningful.
elite.scoring_anchor: 8.5/10 = elite standard. Any domain <8 = priority intervention.
references: Haugen T et al. (2019) PLoS One — elite sprint mechanical variables
Squeo MR et al. (2025) PMC — CPET standards in elite athletes
Buchheit M (2014) Front Physiol — HR monitoring in elite athletes
McMahon JJ et al. (2018) Strength Cond J — CMJ phase analysis

rows.protocol: 3 CMJ trials. Hands on hips. 90 s rest. Impulse-momentum method. Best trial.
rows.notes: Elite athletes. M: 54–72 cm (power sports); F: 42–56 cm. >65 cm (M) = exceptional. NSCA 2024; Haugen et al. (2020).

rows.metric: CMJ Peak Force (relative to body weight)
rows.protocol: Peak GRF (N) ÷ body weight (N). Best of 3 CMJ trials.
rows.notes: Typical ~2.3–3.0× BW in elite power sport athletes. Linthorne (2001); Haugen et al. (2020). >28 N/kg = exceptional.

rows.protocol: Eccentric RFD from braking phase. Mean of 2 best trials.
rows.notes: Elite SSC loading. McMahon et al. (2018). Key return-to-sport criterion post-ACL. Sensitive to neuromuscular fatigue.

rows.protocol: Net concentric impulse from CMJ force-time curve. Takeoff threshold: 10 N.
rows.notes: Impulse-momentum gold standard. r=0.97 with jump height. JSCR.

rows.protocol: Drop jump from 30 cm box. Minimal knee bend. RSI = jump height (m) ÷ GCT (s). Best of 3 trials. 90 s rest.
rows.notes: Elite reactive SSC. Flanagan & Comyns (2008); Beattie et al. (2017). >3.0 m/s = elite power sport threshold.

rows.metric: Squat Jump Peak Force (relative to body weight)
rows.protocol: Static start at 90° knee flexion. 3 SJ trials. No countermovement permitted (monitor on force trace). Peak GRF ÷ body weight.
rows.notes: Concentric-only power. SJ:CMJ ratio <0.85 = SSC deficit. Bosco et al. (1983). Ratio tracks training adaptations.

rows.metric: IMTP Peak Force (relative to body mass)
rows.equipment: STRENGTHSenses GRFP Force Plates + Isometric Rig
rows.protocol: Standardised warm-up. 3 max trials × 3–5 s. Hip 145°, knee ~120°. Net peak force ÷ body mass. Best trial.
rows.notes: Dos'Santos et al. (2017); Comfort et al. (2019). Strength-power: 38–48; Endurance: 28–36 N/kg.

rows.equipment: STRENGTHSenses GRFP Force Plates + Isometric Rig
rows.protocol: 100 ms window from onset (75 N above BW). Mean of 2 best trials.
rows.notes: Haugen et al. (2020). Correlated with 10 m sprint time and CMJ height.

rows.equipment: STRENGTHSenses GRFP Force Plates + Isometric Rig
rows.protocol: 200 ms window from force-time curve.
rows.notes: Later explosive capacity. Tracked alongside 0–100 ms window for full neuromuscular profile. Haff et al. (2015).

rows.protocol: SI% = (|L−R| / max(L,R)) × 100. Dual plates. Mean of 3 CMJ trials.
rows.notes: >6% = investigation, especially post-injury or ACL return-to-sport. Impellizzeri et al. (2007).

rows.protocol: Seated. 3 trials. Best trial. Record both hands and bilateral ratio.
rows.notes: Combat sports: >65 kg. Endurance: 48–55 kg. ACSM norms.

rows.protocol: Multi-plane push/pull power profile. 3 planes × 3 trials. Peak & mean power recorded.
rows.notes: Elite sport-specific power. 3D resistive profiling. Benchmark by sport and position.

rows.protocol: Standardised lift from floor to mid-thigh. Peak force + mean bar velocity recorded. 3 trials.
rows.notes: Functional task-specific strength. Load-velocity profiling for training prescription.

rows.protocol: Sprint from standing start. GCT measured at 5–10 m split (acceleration phase). Mean of ≥6 ground contacts.
rows.notes: Haugen et al. (2020). <140 ms = elite sprint standard. Reflects reactive leg stiffness.

rows.protocol: Peak velocity phase (20–30 m). Mean of ≥6 strides.
rows.notes: Elite sprinters >2.20 m; team sport elites ~1.90–2.10 m. Haugen et al. (2020).

rows.protocol: Sport-specific skill at competition speed. SPARC extracted from primary effector segment.
rows.notes: Elite athletes approach 0 (perfect smoothness). Values near −1.0 = exceptional automaticity. Morin & Samozino (2016).

rows.protocol: Single-leg landing from 30 cm box + 45° cut. 3D knee valgus + hip drop bilateral comparison. Mean of 3 trials each limb.
rows.notes: Elite standard. >5° = priority intervention, especially post-ACL reconstruction.

rows.protocol: T-Mode, 60 s. Standing. 2 scored trials. Best recorded.
rows.notes: Blackwell et al. (2020). Elite athletes 18–40 yr top quartile ~165–200 ms. Age and sex corrections apply.

rows.protocol: Reactive perturbation or drop-landing task. Quad/hamstring onset. Average of 5 trials.
rows.notes: Elite neuromuscular pre-activation. Earlier onset than competitive athletes. Critical for injury prevention.

rows.protocol: Graded exercise test to volitional exhaustion. Individualised ramp protocol. Plateau: ≤2.1 mL/kg/min rise, RER ≥1.10, HR within 10 bpm of predicted max.
rows.notes: Squeo et al. (2025). Elite endurance: 72–92; Power sports: 58–68. Cross-country skiing top tier: >90.

rows.protocol: V-slope method. First systematic rise in VE/VO2 without rise in VE/VCO2.
rows.notes: Elite endurance: VT1:VO2max ratio ~73–78%. Absolute VT1 strongly predicts performance. Buchheit (2014).

rows.protocol: Second deflection. Race pace zone upper boundary. VT2:VO2max ratio tracked.
rows.notes: Elite: VT2 typically >85% VO2max. Critical power proxy. Laursen & Buchheit (2019).

rows.protocol: Peak HR − HR at 60 s passive seated recovery.
rows.notes: Elite: >50 bpm = excellent autonomic fitness. Buchheit (2014); Squeo et al. (2025). Tracks training adaptation longitudinally.

rows.metric: Submaximal HR at Fixed Work Rate
rows.protocol: HR at 200 W (M) or 150 W (F). 5 min steady-state. Tracked longitudinally.
rows.notes: Cardiac adaptation marker. Buchheit (2014). Decline over training blocks = positive aerobic adaptation.

rows.protocol: Pattern Comparison Processing Speed. Age-corrected standard score.
rows.notes: Elite athletes show superior processing speed. NIH Toolbox CB V3 (2025). Track longitudinally for overtraining.

rows.protocol: Flanker Inhibitory Control & Attention. Age-corrected standard score.
rows.notes: Elite sport demands sustained executive function. Longitudinal tracking important for overtraining detection.

rows.protocol: Custom protocol: random + peripheral + moving targets. 90 s. Mean hit time.
rows.notes: Top-quartile Blackwell et al. (2020). <185 ms = exceptional visual-motor response. Age + sex corrected.

rows.protocol: D2 + concurrent sport-specific decision task (opponent reading / tactical stimulus). DTC% derived.
rows.notes: Elite athletes demonstrate near-automaticity. DTC >15% = concern. Monitor across training blocks.

rows.protocol: n-back (2-back) cognitive task during low-intensity exercise (40% HRmax cycling). ΔHbO over PFC channels. 3 min task block. 2 min rest. 3 repetitions.
rows.notes: Greater prefrontal activation under dual physical-cognitive load. Elite athletes show sustained engagement. Scholkmann et al. (2014).

items.text: ACSM Guidelines for Exercise Testing & Prescription, 12th Ed.
Riebe D et al. (2017). Updating ACSM's recommendations for exercise preparticipation health screening. Med Sci Sports Exerc, 47(11), 2473–2479.
Garber CE et al. (2011). Quantity and quality of exercise for developing and maintaining fitness in apparently healthy adults. Med Sci Sports Exerc, 43(7), 1334–1359.
NSCA — Essentials of Strength Training and Conditioning, 4th Ed. Haff GG & Triplett NT (Eds).
Kenney WL, Wilmore JH, Costill DL (2020). Physiology of Sport and Exercise, 7th Ed. Human Kinetics.
Buchheit M & Laursen PB (2013). High-intensity interval training, solutions to the programming puzzle. Part I: cardiopulmonary emphasis. Sports Med, 43, 313–338.
Seiler S (2010). What is best practice for training intensity and duration distribution? Int J Sports Physiol Perform, 5(3), 276–291.
Buchheit M (2014). Monitoring training status with HR measures: do all roads lead to Rome? Front Physiol, 5, 73.
Haugen T et al. (2019). Sprint mechanical variables in elite athletes: are force-velocity profiles sport specific or individual? PLoS One.
Issurin VB (2010). New horizons for the methodology and physiology of training periodization. Sports Med, 40(3), 189–206.
Kiely J (2012). Periodization paradigms in the 21st century: evidence-led or tradition-driven? Int J Sports Physiol Perform, 7(3), 242–250.
NIH Toolbox Cognitive Battery — standardised measures of cognitive function.
Rikli RE & Jones CJ (2013). Senior Fitness Test Manual, 2nd Ed. Human Kinetics.
